message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 25
Recent Green phase: Added the claim subsystem. New types ClaimStep/Damage/Policy/QuoteResult/ClaimResult/StepResult; Step is now QuoteStep|ClaimStep. New constants INSURANCE_VALUE map, DEDUCTIBLE=100, CAP_MULTIPLIER=2. New helpers: insuranceSum(items), damagePayout(damage)=max(amount-100,0), processClaim(policy, claim) which mutates policy.remainingCap and returns {payout, remainingCap}. runScenario now uses a stateful .map with an if/else on step.op: quotes record a policy (cap = insuranceSum*2) keyed by step index and increment quoteCount (used for follow-up = quoteCount>0); claims look up policies[step.policy] and call processClaim. NOTE: payout currently only handles standard reimbursement (no enchantment/dragon clauses, no cap clamping) — those are upcoming tests; do NOT implement them now.

IMPORTANT do NOT write experiment-done.txt — 19 todo tests remain.

Refactor while keeping all tests green. runScenario now has a branch and local mutation (quoteCount, policies[], policy.remainingCap) — evaluate whether the quote-branch body and claim-branch read clearly, consider extracting a recordPolicy helper or similar, evaluate naming, apply Four Rules of Simple Design, and measure APP mass / McCabe / cognitive / ESLint before and after. Keep whichever scores better. Watch the SonarJS cognitive-complexity threshold on runScenario now that it branches.

Run tests with `pnpm test`.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- green: TDD Green Phase
- red: TDD Red Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
25 passing, 19 todo. Now let me measure the refactoring target, `runScenario`.

## PRE-Measurements

**ESLint smells (target file):** 0
**Cognitive complexity warnings:** 0 (none reported, so `runScenario` is below the SonarJS threshold)

**Naming Evaluation (`runScenario`):**
- Purpose: iterate scenario steps, producing a quote result or claim result per step, threading mutable policy state.
- `runScenario` clearly reveals intent. The local `quoteCount > 0` passed as the `isFollowUp` flag is the least clear element — the meaning is buried at the call site.

**APP Mass (`runScenario`):**
- Constants: `0` (CAP_MULTIPLIER ref is binding), literal `0` for quoteCount, `1` for `+= 1` → 2 constants
- Bindings: years, policies, quoteCount, results, scenario.steps, step, index, premium, step.items, step.policy → ~10
- Invocations: yearsWithMHPCO access(0), `.map`, quotePremium, insuranceSum, processClaim → 4
- Conditionals: `step.op === "quote"` (1), `quoteCount > 0` (1) → 2 → mass 8
- Loops: `.map` → 1 → mass 5
- Assignments: `quoteCount = 0`, `quoteCount += 1`, `policies[index] = ...`, `policy.remainingCap -= payout` (in processClaim, not here) → 3 here → mass 18

Rough mass ≈ 2 + 10 + 8 + 8 + 5 + 18 = **51**

**McCabe (`runScenario`):**
- Start: 1
- `if (step.op === "quote")`: +1
- `quoteCount > 0` (boolean expression as branch): +1

McCabe = **3**

The branch body for the quote case does three things (compute premium, increment counter, record policy) inline. Extracting a `recordPolicy` helper would move the policy-construction assignment and the `insuranceSum * CAP_MULTIPLIER` knowledge out of `runScenario`, lowering its mass and tightening the quote branch. Let me apply that single change.

content.text:
Tests still 25/25 green, ESLint still clean.

## POST-Measurements

**`runScenario` after extraction:**

APP Mass:
- Constants: literal `0` (quoteCount), `1` (`+= 1`) → 2
- Bindings: years, policies, quoteCount, results, scenario.steps, step, index, isFollowUp, step.items, step.policy → ~10
- Invocations: yearsWithMHPCO access, `.map`, openPolicy, quotePremium, processClaim → 5 → mass 10
- Conditionals: `step.op === "quote"`, `quoteCount > 0` → 2 → mass 8
- Loops: `.map` → 1 → mass 5
- Assignments: `quoteCount = 0`, `quoteCount += 1`, `policies[index] = ...` → 3 → mass 18

Mass ≈ 2 + 10 + 10 + 8 + 5 + 18 = **53** (vs ~51) — the `openPolicy` invocation added +2, but the `insuranceSum * CAP_MULTIPLIER` invocation+multiply moved out, so the net is roughly flat. The mass that left `runScenario` now lives in the trivially-testable `openPolicy` (mass ≈ items + insuranceSum invocation + multiply + 2 literals ≈ 8).

McCabe (`runScenario`): unchanged at **3** (same two decision points).

**The case for keeping the change despite flat mass/McCabe** — this is a Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) win, not a complexity win:
- `quoteCount > 0` is now named `isFollowUp`, so the follow-up concept is explicit instead of an inline comparison passed positionally.
- Policy construction knowledge (`insuranceSum * CAP_MULTIPLIER`) is now a single named operation `openPolicy`, independently testable, and symmetric with `processClaim` — the two policy-lifecycle operations now read as a pair.
- The quote branch shrank from 6 lines to 4 and each line states one thing.

Mass is flat and McCabe is flat, but clarity improved measurably (named follow-up flag, named policy-open operation) with no test regressions and no new smells. Per the priority order, Rule 2 trumps APP — change kept.

---

## Refactoring Complete

**Refactoring:** Extracted `openPolicy(items)` helper from `runScenario`'s quote branch and named the inline `quoteCount > 0` flag as `isFollowUp`.

**Smell delta (ESLint):** 0 → 0 (clean before and after; no cognitive-complexity warning on `runScenario`, well below SonarJS threshold)
**Cognitive delta (`runScenario`):** below threshold → below threshold (quote branch body simplified)
**APP mass delta (`runScenario`):** ~51 → ~53 (flat; ~8 mass relocated into the testable `openPolicy`)
**McCabe delta (`runScenario`):** 3 → 3 (decision points unchanged)
**Tests:** 25/25 passing (19 todo remain)

The change was a Reveals-Intent improvement rather than a complexity reduction — complexity scores were already at a healthy floor for branching state-threading logic, so the lever available was naming/clarity, which I applied. The `openPolicy`/`processClaim` pair now reads symmetrically.

Implementation file: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-30_01-30-14_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-8-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

Note: 19 todo tests remain — `experiment-done.txt` not written, as instructed.
